title: pH Electrode — Calibration and Millivolt Measurement
content: A glass pH electrode generates a Nernst potential of approximately −59.16 mV per pH unit at 25 °C relative to a reference electrode. Measured output: V_out ≈ E₀ − 59.16 × pH (mV). Two-point calibration (pH 4.01 and pH 7.00 buffer solutions) compensates for electrode offset and slope drift.
extended_content: pH electrodes consist of a pH-sensitive glass membrane (inner solution Ag/AgCl) and a reference electrode (usually calomel or Ag/AgCl). The high electrode impedance (50–500 MΩ) requires an instrumentation amplifier or pH module with buffered input (e.g., DFRobot SEN0161 pH board, Atlas Scientific EZO-pH). Temperature compensation: Nernst slope S = −RT·ln(10)/F ≈ −0.198×T mV/pH (T in Kelvin); at 25 °C S = −59.16 mV/pH. Calibration procedure: immerse in pH 7 buffer → note raw mV as mid_point; immerse in pH 4 buffer → note mV as slope_calibration; pH = (mV − mid_point) / slope_calibration + 7. Electrode storage: keep bulb moist in storage solution (3 M KCl); dry-out permanently damages glass membrane. Response time: < 5 s after equilibration. Lifespan: 1–3 years with proper care.
source: Skoog, Principles of Instrumental Analysis, 7th ed.; DFRobot pH Sensor SEN0161 User Guide
